flags.message: Encountered a CourtListener rate limit (HTTP 429) when attempting to fetch the lower court opinion text using get_endpoint_item. Degraded gracefully to use provisioned inputs and base rates, per instructions.

The cell's corpus query sidecar (127.0.0.1:8377) was unreachable — two `fedcourts query` attempts timed out — so no similar-prior retrieval ran. Degraded, not blocked: the prediction rests on the provisioned snapshot/petition, the committed statpack base rates, and one CourtListener MCP lookup (which surfaced the prior round's cert denial, UMB Bank v. Landmark Towers, No. 19-241). If other cells in this run show the same timeout, the sidecar likely never started.

Corpus query sidecar (127.0.0.1:8377) was unreachable this run — fedcourts query timed out, so no corpus priors informed this cell; predicted from the provisioned inputs, the committed statpack, and CourtListener MCP. Likely affects sibling cells in this fan-out.
Cell was not provisioned with a snapshot (record directory missing). Instructed to assume the case is already decided and predict from base rates without retrieving actual outcome.

Corpus query sidecar unreachable in this cell (fedcourts query timed out against http://127.0.0.1:8377), so no ranked corpus priors were retrieved; the prediction degraded to the committed statpack base rates plus the provisioned inputs, per the prompt contract.

Corpus query sidecar (127.0.0.1:8377) was unreachable (timed out on two attempts), so no similar-prior retrieval via fedcourts query informed this cell; the prediction rests on the provisioned inputs, the committed statpack base rates, and light CourtListener MCP lookups. Degraded, not blocked. Also: the 'Segment base rate by salience band' statpack table referenced by the predict prompt is absent from the committed statpack build.

No docket snapshot, document manifest, or filed-document text was provisioned. The prediction uses only the event definition, forward-mode context, and committed cert base rates; no current-docket or outcome retrieval was attempted.
CourtListener MCP search was rate-limited (HTTP 429) and the local corpus service (fedcourts query) was unreachable. Proceeded with prediction using provisioned documents.
The committed statpack has no salience-band-by-Term table or JSON segment data, so segment_base_rate and brier_skill_score were omitted for all three predictions.

segment_base_rate and brier_skill_score are omitted on all three evaluations this cell: the committed statpack's per-Term salience segments are empty for every Term (metrics/statpack.json terms[].segments == []), and statpack.md carries no 'Segment base rate by salience band' table, so no prior-Term band rate exists to pool for this Term-2025 case. Not a predictor fault; the skill metric will stay null until the statpack emits populated segments.

The provisioned petition.txt is empty (documents.json marks it empty_text: true — a 37-page scanned filing with no text layer), so the questions presented could not be read. The prediction rests on the docket snapshot, CourtListener metadata for the lower-court record, and statpack base rates; given the overwhelmingly negative structural signals (pro se, all responses waived, no CFR, long-conference distribution) the missing text is unlikely to change the call.

The provisioned 32-page petition has empty_text=true because the scan has no extractable text; no questions-presented or brief-in-opposition text is available, so the forecast cannot assess the asserted legal question, claimed split, or vehicle arguments.

Provisioned petition.txt is empty (documents.json: empty_text=true — a scanned filing with no text layer), so the questions presented could not be read. Prediction rests on the docket snapshot, the lower-court record via CourtListener, and corpus base rates.
Snapshot missing. Provisioning likely refused this forward cell because the docket looks terminal. Predicted based on general cert base rates.
No provisioned snapshot or record directory found. Predicting from base rates only per prompt instructions.

Corpus query sidecar (127.0.0.1:8377) was unreachable in this cell — fedcourts query timed out, so no corpus priors were retrieved. Fell back to the committed statpack and provisioned inputs per the degraded-upstream rule; the prediction was not materially affected, but if the outage spans the fan-out, other cells in run 20260718T000130Z were likewise degraded.

The local corpus service (fedcourts query) was unreachable (timed out) during the run, so I could not pull similar historical cases. Proceeded using the provisioned snapshot, general base rates, and knowledge.

CourtListener MCP returned HTTP 429 (1200/day rate limit already exhausted) on this cell's first and only call, at ~00:05 UTC. The cell degraded gracefully (fresh one-day-old snapshot made the lookup confirmatory only), but other cells in this fan-out may be starved of live retrieval entirely.

The predict prompt directs anchoring on the per-Term 'Segment base rate by salience band' table in the committed statpack, but metrics/statpack.md and statpack.json in this checkout carry no such section. Anchored on the Term 2025 paid fee-class grant rate (~5.4%) plus the relist/CVSG cuts instead.

Forward-signal disclosure (decisive): the four companion petitions this petition asks to have consolidated — Health Freedom Defense Fund v. Carvalho (25-765, denied 2026-05-18), Curtis v. Inslee (25-1119), Sweeney v. Univ. of CO Hosp. Auth. (25-1055), and Horsley v. Kaiser Found. (25-1203, all denied 2026-06-01) — were all denied cert without recorded dissent, verified against the supremecourt.gov order-list PDFs. These public, pre-snapshot rulings materially drove the low grant probability. This case's own petition remains pending (response due 2026-08-31); no outcome leakage.

CourtListener MCP sidecar returned HTTP 429 (Rate limit exceeded: 1200/day) on this cell's first tool call, so the shared daily quota was already exhausted when the cell ran; other cells in this fan-out likely hit the same wall. Degraded gracefully to corpus CLI + statpack + public supremecourt.gov order lists per the contract.

A CourtListener lookup found the May 14, 2026 decision in the lead case, Jules v. Andre Balazs Properties. That decision predates the July 17 snapshot, is legitimate forward-mode context, and materially shaped the GVR forecast.

gaps.label: The ranged corpus remote was unreachable because DNS resolution failed
Corpus query sidecar at 127.0.0.1:8377 was unreachable (timed out), so fedcourts query priors were unavailable this run
The 'Segment base rate by salience band' table the predict prompt points to is absent from the committed statpack build
The corpus-prior remote was unreachable because DNS resolution failed.
A 'pending related petitions / same-QP holds' lookup (e.g. petitions citing the same circuit split) would have surfaced Nielsen/Mohan without open-web search

A 'response requested (CFR)' cut in the statpack, analogous to the relist and CVSG cuts — the CFR is the dominant observable signal for this cell and I had to estimate its lift from general knowledge.
A BIO was not yet filed, so nothing to weigh against the petition — expected for a pending case, not a tooling defect.
A CFR (response requested) cut in the statpack would help; it is the single biggest signal on this docket and I had to price it from general knowledge
A base-rate cut conditioned on a Supreme Court response request would improve calibration.
A base-rate cut for pro se (self-represented) paid petitions would sharpen calibration on cells like this one

helpful.label: Provisioned petition and brief in opposition
Complete provisioned petition and brief in opposition
CVSG and fee-class base rates in the committed statpack

gaps: MCP rate limit was exhausted quickly
recent.notes: Had to degrade gracefully to base rates.

gaps: CourtListener MCP search quota was unavailable for all attempted legal-context searches.
helpful: The statpack's Term, paid-petition, originating-circuit, relist, and CVSG cuts materially calibrated the forecast.
The provisioned petition included the full lower-court opinion, allowing vehicle-quality assessment without live retrieval.
recent.notes: The complete provisioned filing and committed base rates supported a clean degraded-mode prediction despite the MCP throttle.

gaps: The statpack lacks the salience-band section the predict prompt tells predictors to anchor on — either ship the section or drop the instruction
No statpack cut for response-waived vs response-filed petitions (or CFR incidence), which is the strongest docket-posture signal at this stage
One unfiltered fedcourts query timed out against the corpus sidecar (http://127.0.0.1:8377); narrower filtered queries succeeded
helpful: Full petition text plus extracted questions-presented made the merits/vehicle assessment possible without any document fetching
statpack.json per-fee-class grant rates (paid vs IFP) were the single most useful quantitative cut for a paid petition
Granted-prior rows from fedcourts query exposed the multiple-distribution/relist pattern that contextualizes this case's single long-conference distribution
recent.notes: Forward cell with a fresh (same-day) snapshot; no BIO exists because the government waived response, so the petition-vs-BIO weighing the prompt describes was not applicable.

gaps: The statpack.md file did not contain the 'Segment base rate by salience band' table mentioned in the instructions, requiring fallback to individual cuts like relist count and fee class.
helpful: The corpus stats on fee class (paid vs IFP) and relists were very helpful for anchoring the base rate.
CourtListener MCP search revealed the circuit split context quickly.
recent.notes: Good tooling overall. The rate limiting on the MCP server required a short sleep before retrying.

gaps: CourtListener MCP was temporarily unavailable because its daily rate limit was exhausted.
The corpus query surface cannot search SCOTUS priors by legal issue, so its returned grant-side priors were only general context.
helpful: The complete petition and appendix exposed both the textual argument and the absence of a direct circuit split.
Fee-class, originating-circuit, relist, and CVSG base rates supported calibrated certiorari odds.
recent.notes: The provisioned record was sufficient for a prediction despite the live-research degradation. A provisioned response brief would have improved adversarial assessment, but the docket reflects that respondents waived response.

gaps: statpack has no salience-band table (the prompt references one) and no fee-class (paid vs IFP) grant-rate cut in the markdown; had to reason about the paid-petition adjustment qualitatively.
`fedcourts query --era modern` silently returns zero rows; the era vocabulary (2020s, 1990s, ...) is only discoverable from the statpack.
No easy corpus cut for 'respondent waived response / call-for-response' status, which is one of the strongest deny-side signals at the petition stage.
helpful: Full petition text with the lower-court orders as appendices made the vehicle/split analysis possible without any docket fetches.
Statpack relist-count and per-circuit cuts gave a concrete anchor for a zero-relist, waived-response petition.
recent.notes: Forward cell; ~4 retrieval calls total, well under budget.

helpful: The committed statpack was sufficient to establish the extremely low base rate for this type of petition.
recent.notes: No live corpus queries or CourtListener MCP calls were necessary for this straightforward state bar discipline denial.

gaps: Corpus query has no keyword or semantic similarity filter
CourtListener MCP daily throttling prevented a targeted attorney-discipline search
helpful: Complete provisioned petition text and docket snapshot
CourtListener searches for general fair-warning doctrine
recent.notes: The provisioned record was sufficient to finish cleanly. Corpus queries were useful for structural priors but not doctrinal analogues.
